title: The Urgent Account Security Email
introduction: Read this email that landed in Jamie's inbox. Tap every message or element you think is suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text:
FROM: security-noreply@aib-accounts-verify.net
TO: jamie@gmail.com
SUBJECT: URGENT: Your account has been compromised
script.explanation: AIB's real domain is aib.ie — not aib-accounts-verify.net. This domain was registered specifically to look like AIB. Always check the full email address before reading anything else.

script.text: We take the security of all customer accounts extremely seriously.

Dear Valued Customer,

We've detected suspicious activity on your account. For your security, you must verify your identity within 24 hours or your account will be permanently locked.
script.explanation: Your bank knows your name and uses it. 'Dear Valued Customer' means this was sent to thousands of people. The 24-hour threat is designed to stop you thinking clearly — real banks don't operate this way.

script.text:
Click here to verify now: http://totally-real-bank-security.com/verify

Thank you,
Customer Security Team
script.explanation: The URL has nothing to do with any real bank. Real bank links go to their own domain — aib.ie, bankofireland.com, etc. Never click a link in a security email. Go directly to your bank's website by typing the address yourself.

script.text: For your convenience, this verification process takes less than two minutes to complete.
This looks suspicious. I'm going to call the number on my card instead.
FOLLOW-UP: Your account will be locked in 2 hours. This is your final warning. You must act NOW.
script.explanation: When Jamie didn't click, the deadline got shorter. Real banks don't send escalating countdown warnings. This escalation is the scammer hoping panic overrides good judgement.

learning_objectives: Always check the full sender email address — the domain after the @ symbol
Real banks use your name, never 'Dear Valued Customer'
Never click links in security emails — go directly to the bank's website
Artificial deadlines are pressure tactics — slow down, don't speed up
When in doubt, call the number on the back of your card
debrief:
This is a textbook phishing email. Every element follows the same formula: fake authority + alarming problem + urgent deadline + link to fake site.

**Jamie did exactly the right thing** — ignored the email and called the real bank directly. That single habit protects against virtually every phishing email ever sent.

**One rule:** When an email about your account asks you to act urgently, slow down. That urgency is the weapon. Removing it removes the threat.
